common_name: Moorpark Apricot (Classic English Heirloom Apricot, Large-Fruited Heritage Apricot)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5), deep, fertile soil in full sun; Moorpark requires approximately 700–900 chill hours and a cross-pollinator — it is not reliably self-fruitful — pair with Harcot, Blenheim, or another compatible apricot; best adapted to zones 5–8 where adequate winter cold accumulates and late spring frosts do not regularly destroy the early-blooming blossoms.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Moorpark requires a cross-pollinator; thin heavy crops to 4–6 inches after natural fruit drop for maximum size; harvest when fruit is deep orange, fully colored, and gives slightly to pressure — Moorpark is at its best consumed fresh within days of picking, as the extraordinarily rich, honeyed flavor at peak ripeness is what makes this variety historically important. Moorpark apricot is one of the great classical apricot varieties of the English-speaking world, an heirloom dating to at least the early 1600s in England and named for Moorpark House in Hertfordshire where it was grown extensively — though its origins may be even earlier and possibly Italian or Continental European. For centuries, Moorpark was the standard of apricot excellence in England, appearing in historic orchards of country houses and kitchen gardens throughout the British Isles, and was the variety which set the benchmark against which all other apricots were evaluated. The fruits are large (for the era), round to oval, deep orange to orange-red with a distinctive dark blush, with firm, richly sweet-tart, intensely flavored orange flesh that is considered among the finest flavors of any apricot variety even by modern standards — a rich, honeyed, complex flavor that distinguishes it from the milder, blander commercial varieties selected for shelf life. Moorpark is not self-fertile and requires cross-pollination; the spring blossoms appear very early and are vulnerable to late frosts, making site selection on a frost-protected slope or south-facing wall important for reliable fruiting.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Moorpark Apricot Heritage Variety
Penn State Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide

common_name: Blenheim Apricot (Royal Blenheim, California Standard Apricot, Santa Clara Valley Apricot)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, deep, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Blenheim is self-fertile and requires no cross-pollinator; it has a low chill requirement (approximately 400–600 hours, among the lowest of any standard apricot) making it particularly well-adapted to the mild winters of coastal California, the Sacramento Valley, and similar zone 7–9 climates where other apricots with higher chill requirements perform unreliably.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Blenheim is self-fertile and drought-tolerant once established; thin to 3–4 inches after June drop; harvest when fruits are deep golden-orange and begin to soften — Blenheim must be harvested and eaten within days of peak ripeness, as the aromatic, intensely flavored flesh does not hold. Blenheim (sold commercially as 'Royal Blenheim' to distinguish it from Moorpark, with which it shares British origins) was the commercial apricot standard of California for over a century, dominating the Santa Clara Valley apricot industry that made California's 'Apricot Capital' from the 1880s through the mid-20th century before urban development replaced the orchards. The variety's characteristics — low chill requirement, self-fertility, reliable production in California's mild winters, and exceptional flavor — made it ideal for the Central Valley and Bay Area climates where it had no commercial equal. The fruits are medium-sized, golden-orange with a red blush, with rich, highly aromatic, intensely flavored flesh that is considered by many apricot enthusiasts to be the most complex-flavored of any commercial apricot variety — a concentrated sweetness with floral, honeyed notes and classic apricot fragrance at peak ripeness that far surpasses the flavor of supermarket apricots harvested underripe for shipping. Blenheim's low chill requirement (400–600 hours) makes it a good choice for zone 7–9 gardeners where higher-chill varieties like Moorpark and Harcot may not receive adequate winter cold.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Apricot Varieties
Sacramento Valley Agricultural Notes — Blenheim Apricot

common_name: Harcot Apricot (Canadian Disease-Resistant Apricot, Northeast-Adapted Apricot)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, deep fertile soil in full sun on a protected slope or elevated site to minimize spring frost risk; Harcot is self-fertile and requires no cross-pollinator; it requires approximately 800–900 chill hours and is one of the most cold-hardy named apricot varieties available, making it the premier recommendation for northeastern United States and Canadian gardens where standard California varieties lack sufficient winter hardiness or cold resistance in the buds.

directions: Plant on a north-facing slope, elevated ground, or north side of a windbreak to delay bloom and reduce late spring frost damage to blossoms; Harcot is self-fertile; thin fruits to 4–5 inches after natural drop; fruit ripens in July producing medium to large, firm, orange fruits with very good sweet-tart flavor and above-average resistance to brown rot — a critical advantage in humid northeastern climates. Harcot apricot was released by Agriculture Canada's Harrow Research Station in Ontario in 1977 as part of a breeding program aimed at developing apricot varieties with improved cold-hardiness and disease resistance for production in eastern Canada and the northeastern United States, where the combination of cold winters, late spring frosts, and humid summer conditions had historically made reliable apricot production difficult or impossible. Harcot achieved a combination of characteristics that made it the premier recommended apricot for the northeastern states and eastern Canada: significantly better cold-hardiness than California-bred varieties (surviving winters in zone 4), self-fertility requiring no companion tree, above-average resistance to the brown rot disease that devastates apricots in humid climates, and good fruit quality — medium to large fruits with firm, orange flesh, a pleasant sweet-tart balanced flavor, and good fresh-eating quality. University cooperative extension services throughout the northeastern states from New England through the Mid-Atlantic list Harcot as their primary or sole recommended apricot variety for home orchards in the region, reflecting its exceptional combination of hardiness, disease tolerance, and quality under northeastern conditions.
sources: Agriculture Canada — Harrow Research Station, Harcot release
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Apricot Production in New York
Penn State Extension — Tree Fruit Production Guide

common_name: Gold Kist Apricot (Low-Chill Southern Apricot, Gulf South Apricot)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter to early spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Gold Kist has one of the lowest chill requirements of any named apricot (approximately 300 hours below 45°F), making it the standard recommended apricot for the mild-winter Gulf South, central Texas, the San Joaquin Valley, and similar climates where higher-chill varieties like Moorpark and Harcot receive insufficient winter cold to bloom and fruit reliably; self-fertile.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Gold Kist is self-fertile and drought-tolerant once established; thin fruits to 3–4 inches after natural fruit drop; Gold Kist ripens early (May in the Gulf South and central Texas), one of the earliest-ripening apricots; harvest when fully orange and beginning to soften for best flavor. Gold Kist apricot was developed by Zaiger Genetics in California and released in the mid-1980s specifically to address the near-absence of suitable apricot varieties for mild-winter regions — the Gulf South, South Texas, the lower San Joaquin Valley, and other areas where winter temperatures rarely accumulate the 700+ chill hours required by standard apricot varieties. With a chill requirement of approximately 300 hours, Gold Kist dramatically expanded the geographic range of apricot production in the United States, particularly through the deep South and the Gulf Coast where no practical apricot had previously been available. The fruits are medium-sized, golden-orange to light orange at full ripeness, with good sweet-tart apricot flavor — somewhat milder than the finest California varieties like Blenheim but very acceptable for fresh eating and preserves. The early ripening season (May in zone 8–9) is an important characteristic: Gold Kist delivers fresh apricots weeks before any other stone fruit of comparable quality, giving it a clear market window at farmers' markets and roadside stands. It remains the primary recommendation from Texas A&M AgriLife Extension and Louisiana State University's AgCenter for home garden apricot production in the Gulf South.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Fruit Varieties for Texas
University of California Cooperative Extension — Low-Chill Stone Fruits

common_name: Tilton Apricot (California Canning Apricot, Late-Season Commercial Apricot)

planting.note: Plant bare-root in late winter in deep, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Tilton is self-fertile and a reliable heavy producer; it requires approximately 600–700 chill hours and is well-adapted to the San Joaquin Valley, the Pacific Northwest, and similar zones 6–9 climates; it blooms later than other apricots, making it somewhat more frost-resistant in regions with unreliable late-spring temperatures.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Tilton is self-fertile; thin to 3–5 inches after June drop; Tilton ripens mid-to-late season (July in California), about 2 weeks later than Blenheim, providing a fresh apricot harvest window after the main-season varieties finish; firm flesh holds quality after picking and is excellent for drying, canning, and jam. Tilton apricot is a California variety with origins in the San Joaquin Valley community of Tilton (now part of Hanford), from which it takes its name, and it was one of the primary commercial canning apricot varieties in California's Central Valley from the late 19th century through much of the 20th century alongside Blenheim. Where Blenheim is the benchmark for fresh-eating quality, Tilton became the commercial preference for processing — its firmer flesh holds structure better through the heat of canning and cooking than the softer, more aromatic Blenheim, producing intact apricot halves that maintain attractive color and firmness in the can. Tilton fruits are medium-sized, round to oval, pale orange to golden-orange with a slight reddish blush, with firm, moderately sweet, mildly tart flesh and a pleasant though not exceptional apricot flavor. The variety blooms somewhat later than Blenheim and Moorpark — an advantage in regions with late spring frost risk — and the firm flesh makes it better for shipping and home drying into dried apricots. A solid production variety for home orchards where a large canning crop is desired.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Apricot Varieties
Washington State University Extension — Tree Fruit Production
California Department of Food and Agriculture — Stone Fruit Industry Reports
